coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of bright blue-emitting carbon dots for bioimaging and fluorescent determination of baicalein
coredata.dc:creator: Luo, Jiabao
Sun, Zishan
Zhou, Weiying
Mo, Fuwang
Wu, Zhan-chao
Zhang, Xinguo
coredata.dc:description: Bright blue-emitting CDs (B-CDs) with a considerable QY of 66 % were synthesized from precursors of cysteine, trisodium citrate dehydrate and ethylenediamine by a simple hydrothermal method. Excellent luminescence stability with various pH, salt concentrations, storage time and UV radiation time is found with excitation-independent emission. Based on the good biocompatibility, B-CDs was successfully used for fluorescence imaging of human ovarian cancer cells. Moreover, the B-CDs were confirmed to be capable of baicalein sensing with a good linear relationship in range of 0–30 μM and the limit of detection of 33 nM. The corresponding sensing mechanism was proved to be fluorescence internal filtration effect and the static quenching effect. All results demonstrate that the B-CDs have good potential of the versatile utilization in biology-relevant fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Bioimaging
Baicalein
Luminescent sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of blue-emitting N, S-doped carbon dots
Determination of baicalein in biological samples
Biocompatibility evaluation and cell imaging of B-CDs
Optimization of sensing conditions for baicalein
Mechanism of B-CDs in baicalein sensing
Selective ability of B-CDs toward baicalein in real sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Transmission electron microscope image of B-CDs and particle size distribution of B-CDs. (b) FT-IR spectrum of B-CDs.
(a) The total X-ray photoelectron spectrum of B-CDs. (b–d) high-resolution C1s, N1s and S2s of B-CDs, respectively.

(a) Excitation spectra and emission spectra of 100 g/mL B-CDs aqueous solutions, (b) +emission spectra of 100 g/mL B-CDs aqueous solutions at different excitation wavelengths, (c) fluorescence spectra of B-CDs aqueous solutions at different concentrations, and (d) fluorescence lifetime spectra of B-CDs aqueous solutions.

(a) The fluorescence spectrum of B-CDs in 0 mM–400 mM NaCl solution, (b) the fluorescence spectrum of B-CDs in the range of pH = 2–12, (c) the fluorescence spectrum of B-CDs in the range of pH = 2–12 Fluorescence spectra of B-CDs before and after being irradiated with a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp for 24 h, (d) Fluorescence spectra of B-CDs before and after storage for 90 days.

(a) Cell viability of SKOV-3 cells in the presence of different concentrations of B-CDs. (b) From left to right: Cell imaging of SKOV-3 human ovarian cancer cells co-incubated with B-CDs of 200 μg/mL for 1 h, 2 h, 4 h, 6 h and 8 h; From top to bottom: B-CDs channel, bright field channel, and merged channel image.

Effect of different conditions on baicalin detection by B-CDs (F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs before and after the addition of baicalein respectively): (a) pHvalue of PBS buffer, (b) concentration of B-CDs.
Response of 5 μg/mL B-CDs fluorescence spectrum to different concentrations of baicalein in the system, (a) 2D spectra and 3D spectra, (b) Lineweaver Burks curve of baicalein detection by B-CDs.

(a) UV absorption of baicalein and excitation-emission spectra of B-CDs, (b) Fluorescence decay lifetime of B-CDs with different concentrations of baicalein, (c) Effect of baicalein at different concentrations on B-CDs’ UV absorption, (d) Effect of 10 μM baicalein on B-CDs’ UV absorption.

(a) Effects of common metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of the B-CDs/baicalein system. (b) Effects of different common biological molecules on the fluorescence intensity of the cys-CD/baicalein system.
Recovery of baicalein by cys-CD in human urine and human serum samples.

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of bright blue-emitting carbon dots for bioimaging and fluorescent determination of baicalein

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of New Drug Screening

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bright blue-emitting CDs (B-CDs) with a considerable QY of 66 % were synthesized from precursors of cysteine, trisodium citrate dehydrate and ethylenediamine by a simple hydrothermal method. Excellent luminescence stability with various pH, salt concentrations, storage time and UV radiation time is found with excitation-independent emission. Based on the good biocompatibility, B-CDs was successfully used for fluorescence imaging of human ovarian cancer cells. Moreover, the B-CDs were confirmed to be capable of baicalein sensing with a good linear relationship in range of 0–30 μM and the limit of detection of 33 nM. The corresponding sensing mechanism was proved to be fluorescence internal filtration effect and the static quenching effect. All results demonstrate that the B-CDs have good potential of the versatile utilization in biology-relevant fields.

ce:section.ce:para: As a novel fluorescent nanomaterial, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted tremendous attentions owing to their predominant properties, such as excellent fluorescence properties, favorable biocompatibility, photobleaching resistance, ease of surface functionalization, low toxicity and good water solubility [1–5]. These remarkable properties endow CDs with multitudinous applications such as catalysis [6,7], biological imaging [8], photoelectric equipment [9] and fluorescent sensors [10–12]. Among the applications of CDs referred, biological imaging and fluorescent sensors are the most studied area owning to its good biocompatibility and the fluorescence intensity are easily changed when CDs interact with different substance or under different environmental factors. The tunable optical property of CDs, which is associated with their particle size, surface functional groups and excitation radiative recombination, has broadened their application in the field of fluorescent sensors [13]. Therefore, CDs is an ideal fluorescent nanomaterial as fluorescent sensors in fields like chemical detection/sensing and biological imaging [14–17]. To date, many carbon dots have been successfully synthesized and explored as bioimaging and fluorescent sensors for detecting Fe3+ [18], Hg2+ [19], 2,4,6-trinitrophenol [20], toxic organic pollutants [21] and so on, but the excellent sensors with high sensitivity and selectivity toward analysis, high quantum yield, photostability and biocompatibility are in short supply [22].

Baicalein (5,6,7-trihydroxyflflavone), as the main ingredients of traditional Chinese medicine Scutellaria baicalensis Georgi, occupies an important position in prevention and treatment of physical health owing to its different medicinal values, such as anti-bacterial [23] Anti-tumor [24], antiviral [25]and anti-oxidant [26] et al. However, excessive supplementation of baicalein will resulting in serious side effects including spasm, dizziness and coma [27]. Hence, it is of great significance to develop new technique with greater precision, sensitivity and convenience to detect the concentration of baicalein. Up to date, varieties of analytical techniques for the analysis of baicalein have been developed, including voltammetry [28,29], UV–vis spectrophotometry [30], thin layer chromatography (TLC) [31,32], liquid chromatography/tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [33], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [34] and fluorescence spectroscopy [35]. Despite the fact that these analytical methods show high sensitivity, selectivity and accuracy, most of them show inevitable defects like expensive, time-consuming and need of complicated sample pretreatment. Obviously, these disadvantages can limit their capability in detecting the baicalein in clinical analysis. Therefore, developing novel simple and effective approaches for detecting baicalein has great positive significance. Based on the excellent properties of CDs, especially the co-doped CDs, it is of great interest to fabricate a fluorescent probe for quantitative detection of baicalein. To the best of our knowledge, it has been reported in the literature referring to the application of CDs towards the detection of baicalein, but the fluorescence quantum yield of their synthesized CDs is just 49%, which is not high enough. It is reported that high quantum efficiency is closely correlated to strong fluorescent intensity, which plays important role to both sensitively sensing various notable analytes wide dynamic range and low LODs and bioimaging [36].

In this work, l-cysteine, trisodium citrate dihydrate and ethylenediamine was used to prepare the B-CDs by simple hydrothermal method, which possesses high blue-fluorescence with excellent stability at various pH, storage time and ionic strength, good biocompatibility and high fluorescence quantum yield reached to 66 %. In addition, B-CDs has the capacity of imaging of human ovarian cancer cells and detecting the baicalein in high selectivity with the existence of interference, proving that the B-CDs is of great potential of the versatile utilization in biology-relevant fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of blue-emitting N, S-doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: A solution of l-cysteine (0.2 g) and trisodium citrate dehydrate (0.2 g) in 10 mL deionized water was added slowly anhydrous ethylenediamine (500 μL), the resulting mixture was homogenized by treatment of ultraphonic about 5 min. The uniformly mixed colorless and transparent solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C in an electric blast drying oven for 4 h. The reaction system was cool to room temperature after it was completed. Then, the mixture was filtered by 0.22 mm membrane to remove larger particles, and followed by purification with a dialysis membrane with MWCO of 1000 Da at 4 °C for 48 h to attain B-CDs, which were stored at 4 °C for subsequent analysis.

Tecnai F20 field emission microscope was used for the Morphological evaluation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was carried out by using X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (ESCALAB 250Xi) and PerKin EImer spectrometer, respectively. Fluorescence measurements were performed by Edinburgh FLS980 spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was recorded by Tecnai F20 field emission microscope. UV–Vis absorption spectrum was observed by Metash UV-3600 spectrophotometer and the fluorescence images of cells were obtained by LSM 880 with Airyscan confocal Laser confocal microscopy.

1 mL of SKOV-3 cells was added to a 15 mm glass-bottom culture dish for inoculation. After culturing for 12 h in a 37 °C, 5 % CO2 constant temperature incubator, the old medium was aspirated and add 1 mL of B-CDs medium solution (200 μg/mL), cultured for 1 h, 2 h, 4 h, 6 h, 8 h, respectively. After the incubation, the drug solution was removed, followed by the addition of 2 mL of 4% paraformaldehyde, which was washed with PBS (pH = 7.4) after standing for 20 min. Finally, anti-fluorescence quenching mounting tablets was appended. Images were captured with a confocal microscope, where the emission intensity of B-CDs was recorded at 488 nm excitation. Imaging results was observed under an inverted confocal laser microscope.

B-CDs aqueous solution (50 μg/mL, 500 μL), absolute ethanol (2 mL), PBS buffer (pH = 7.4, 2 mL) was mixed and Shake well, then different concentration of baicalein ethanol solution varying from 0 μM to 30 μM was added respectively. After shaking, standing for 10 min, the solutions were used to test the fluorescence spectrum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of baicalein in biological samples
ce:section.ce:para: Urine samples and blood samples of healthy volunteers were used as the research objects to investigate the recovery rate of baicalein. The sample pretreatment process is as follows: the urine is centrifuged for 15 min (8000 rpm/min) to obtain the supernatant, which is diluted with ultra-pure water for 500 times and refrigerated for further application, plasma was mixed with acetonitrile in a 1:1 ratio, then standing for 5 min and centrifuging for 10 min (10000 rpm/min) to attain supernatant, then filtered using Polyethersulfone filter membrane with a diameter of 0.45 μm, diluted with PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) and refrigerated for later use. Standard addition method was used to add standard solution of baicalein to blood and urine samples to investigate the recovery rate of standard addition.

The morphology of the B-CDs is observed by TEM and depicted in Fig. 1 a, they are spherical objects with good dispersion, with particle size ranging from 2.7 nm to 4.9 nm and the average particle size is 3.82 nm. The surface groups and chemical composition of B-CDs were analyzed by infrared spectroscopy (Fig. 1b). In Fig. 1b, the broad peaks at 3420 cm−1 and 3125 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibration absorption peaks of O–H and N–H respectively [36], the characteristic absorption peaks of C–N and –SH are located near 2400 cm−1; the CO stretching vibration peak at 1660 cm−1 and the C–N stretching vibration peak at 1401 cm−1 [37]; The 1293 cm−1 peak is about the characteristic absorption peak of C–S [38]. The above data illustrates that nitrogen and sulfur have successfully co-doped in the CDs.

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopies (XPS) of B-CDs are shown in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2a shows the binding energy peaks at 531 eV, 398 eV, 283 eV, and 152 eV belong to O (1s), N (1s), C (1s), and S (2p), respectively, which demonstrates that B-CDs is mainly composed of O, C, N, S [39]. Further, Fig. 2b–d exhibit high-resolution C1s, N1s and S2s of B-CDs, respectively. Fig. 2b is the spectrum of C1s, which depicted three peaks corresponding to C–C at 283 eV, CC at 284 eV and C–N/C–O at 286 eV. Fig. 2c, as N1s spectrum, in which can be seen that there are two peaks at 398 eV, 399 eV belonging to pyridinic N and amino N, separately [40]. The S2s spectrum is put in Fig. 2d, showing an asymmetric peak at 167.6 eV, which is caused by a -C-SO x - (x = 2, 3, 4) species corresponding to 167.6, 168.5, and 169.3 eV, respectively [41]. Combining the characterization data of infrared spectroscopy and XPS, a conclusion can be drawn that B-CDs obtained by hydrothermal synthesis is successfully doped with nitrogen and sulfur, but still retains certain structural characteristics of l-cysteine, which has luxuriant functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, amino, and carboxyl groups on the surface, providing more options and possibilities for subsequent modifications, transformations and applications.

As can be seen in Fig. 3 a, B-CDs exhibits a broad emission band peaked at 442 nm at optimal 350 nm excitation, which can be put down to π-π* transition of CO and CN bond in the carbon dot structure [42]. Optimal 100 μg/mL B-CDs aqueous solution is a bright yellow transparent liquid under natural light, while emits bright blue fluorescence under UV irradiation (Fig. 3a inset). The corresponding quantum yield was measured to be 66 % using quinine sulfate as standard, which is higher than many reported carbon dots [35,43].

Fig. 3b exhibits the emission spectrum excited by different excitation wavelengths changing from 325 nm to 395 nm, in which the highest fluorescence intensity was obtained at 350 nm excitation. Nevertheless, the maximum emission peak at 442 nm remains unchanged, demonstrates its excitation-independent behavior and indicates the particle size and surface states of B-CDs is uniform [44,45], which is conducive to the subsequent application. The fluorescence spectra of B-CDs aqueous solutions with different concentrations under excitation at 365 nm are tested and exhibited in Fig. 3c, In the concentration range of 50 μg/mL to 200 μg/mL, the emission intensity of B-CDs increases in proportion to the concentration. Luminescent concentration quenching of B-CDs occurs in the interval of 200 μg/mL-2000 μg/mL, which might be attributed to aggregation induced quenching effect when B-CDs aqueous solutions is of high concentration. Hence, the most ideal blue fluorescence can achieve with the concentration of B-CDs aqueous solution arranging from 100 μg/mL to 200 μg/mL in a neutral environment. Fluorescence lifetime was measured with 320 nm laser and monitoring 445 nm emission (Fig. 3d) and calculated to be 10.57 ns.

Since a large number of carbon dots have been synthesized through citric acid and ethylenediamine [46–49], the correlation of optical property among the as-prepared CDs and previous reported CDs deserve further investigation. Firstly, most of the previous reported CDs emit blue fluorescence under excitation around 350 nm, possessing high quantum yield ranging from 50 % to 98 % and excellent photostability. Hence, they have been widely developed for fluorescent probes and biological imaging. As for fluorescent probes, most of them were applied to sensing notable ion in our daily life. The reason for it is that there exits abundant carbonyl, amine groups on the surface of carbon dots, which have a high affinity to ion, upon they combine into a complex, charge transfer effect can cause the decrease in fluorescence intensity. Reasonably, our prepared B-CDs retain similar optical property with high quantum yield and excellent photostability confirmed by the experiment mentioned below. The high quantum yield could be ascribed to the doping of N atoms, which generates more electron-enriched active sites and promotes the high yield of radiative recombination with facilitate the decrease in non-radiative recombination [50].

Pertaining to the accurate chemical structure of the emission center of CDs, the most possible reason is that the existence of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon or small molecular fluorophores as fluorescent center [51–53], which are closely related to different raw materials and synthesis conditions for carbon dots. Even using the same raw materials, the fluorescence center of CDs will change at the various synthesis condition. For example, Wang [52] synthesized CDs hydrothermally using citric acid and urea under various reaction time and temperature. Then, they surprisingly found that polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon domains mainly contribute to PL at high synthesis temperatures, while small molecular fluorophores dominate the PL at shorter reaction time and lower synthesis temperatures. Besides, several reports [54–56] have shown that using citric acid and an amine-based nitrogen molecule can synthesize N-CDs containing blue-emitting fluorophores. When the amine-based nitrogen molecule is ethylenediamine, the molecular fluorophores comes to be IPCA [54]. Differently, both polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon domains and molecular fluorophores contribute to PL of N-CDs using citric acid and ethylenediamine as precursor, which is illustrated by investigating their structure, UV absorption spectrum, fluorescence lifetime, QY and so on [51].

There is no doubt that the emission center of our B-CDs needs to be confirmed by analyzing their structure and optical pattern. Firstly, its ultraviolet absorption spectrum (Fig. 8c), excitation and emission spectra (Fig. 3a), QY (66%) and fluorescence lifetime spectrum (Fig. 8b) are used to compared with IPCA's, which exhibits the similar excitation and emission spectra and the same absorption at 250 nm and 350 nm, but stronger absorption at 250 nm and have no absorption at 400–450 nm. The corresponding PL QY and the decay time is 86% and 14 ns, respectively. Considering our B-CDs’ excitation-independent luminescence, which can perfectly be explained by molecular fluorophores theory, a conclusion can be drawn that emission center of our B-CDs is partially attributed to IPCA [56]. In the other hand, the shortened fluorescence lifetime and slight absorption at 400–450 nm complies with the description in the Ehrat's [54] report that both of them are mainly due to the form of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon domains. Thus, B-CDs most likely possesses two luminous centers: IPCA and polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon domains.

The possible impact factors including pH, salt concentrations, storage time and radiation time on luminescent stability of B-CDs was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the fluorescence intensity of 100 μg/mL B-CDs aqueous solution at the Na+ and Cl-concentration arranging from 0 nm to 400 nm remains basically unchanged, suggesting that the B-CDs fluorescence is of excellent salt stability [57]. The pH-dependent emission spectra of B-CDs are depicted in Fig. 4b, in which the highest emission intensity is found at pH = 7. It can maintain good blue fluorescence performance under weak acid and weak base environments, though it decreases significantly with maximum emission red shift under strong acid conditions (pH < 3) and decreases dramatically under strong alkaline conditions (pH > 11), which is related to the fact that the surface groups of the carbon dots are prone to hydrolysis under strong acid or alkali conditions, resulting in significant changes in fluorescence properties. After being irradiated by a UV lamp (365 nm, 12 W) for 24 h (Fig. 4c) the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs aqueous solution has no significance change, indicating that it possesses good stability and ideal anti-photobleaching effect [58]. As illustrated in Fig. 4d, the fluorescence of B-CDs does not change significantly under the condition of storage at 4 °C for 90 days, proving that it has long-term stable fluorescence performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility evaluation and cell imaging of B-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the excellent fluorescence property of B-CDs, biocompatibility was evaluated by detecting the cytotoxicity toward SKOV-3 cells using CCK8 assay [59]. The viability rate of SKOV-3 cells cultivated with various concentrations of B-CDs was investigated and depicted in Fig. 5 a. Even the SKOV-3 cells were incubated with B-CDs at a high concentration of 125 μg/mL for 24 h, the cells remained viability rate of more than 90 %, demonstrating that B-CDs has low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility, which ensures its versatile utilization in biology-relevant fields.

Owing to good biocompatibility and excellent fluorescence property, B-CDs is used for cell imaging, and 200 μg/mL solution of B-CDs was used as the concentration of cell imaging (the cell survival rate of SKOV-3 human ovarian cancer cells was still over 80% after incubation for 24 h at this concentration). Fig. 5b shows B-CDs channel, bright field channel, and merged channel image of the laser confocal microscope (λ ex = 405 nm, λ em = 445 nm). In the first 6 h, B-CDs entered the cells gradually to endow the cells with blue fluorescence, and the fluorescence intensity inside the cells enhances with incubating time prolonging. Meanwhile, B-CDs accumulate in the cell nucleus increasingly and causes the nucleus to emit bright blue fluorescence, which indicates that the particle size of B-CDs is less than 10 nm and has the imaging ability of nucleus. After 8 h, blue fluorescence intensity in the cell has been significantly reduced, which might be due to the fact that B-CDs is actively excreted and intracellular uptake by cells and leads to the decrease of intracellular fluorescence. Briefly, microscope image shows that B-CDs have good imaging ability in cells, compared with organic fluorescent dyes with obvious photobleaching effect and fluorescent quantum dots with strong biological toxicity and heavy metal leakage [60,61].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of sensing conditions for baicalein
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on the fluorescence property of B-CDs, its potential of fluorescent detection of baicalein is exploded by its PL spectra variation. Firstly, the optimal conditions on detecting baicalein were investigated, which is showed in Fig. 6 (F 0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs before and after the addition of baicalein, respectively). With changing pH value, the F 0 and F 0–F show apparent changes and reach the maximum value at pH = 7–8 (Fig. 6a), proving that B-CDs has the most obvious response to baicalein in this range. In view of the fact that pH = 7.4 is commonly used as the buffer system in biological systems, the PBS buffer solution with pH = 7.4 is selected for detecting baicalein in subsequent experiments. The B-CDs concentration-dependence F
0
/F on sensing baicalein is depicted in Fig. 6b, which exhibits the response of 3–7 μg/mL B-CDs to 0–20 μM baicalein. The linear fitting result of F
0
/F and baicalein concentration shows that both the linear fitting slope and the detection sensitivity reach the highest at 5 μg/mL B-CDs. In summary, the optimal sensing condition is that pH = 7.4 and the B-CDs’ concentration is 5 μg/mL B-CDs.

The optimal condition mentioned above was employed for detecting baicalein, and the detection results are shown in Fig. 7 a. The fluorescence of B-CDs showed a regular downward trend with increasing baicalein content. Taking the integrated intensity of B-CDs corresponding to each baicalein concentration as the object of investigation, the Lineweaver Burks curve can be used to demonstrates the linear relationship between them (Fig. 7b). The reciprocal of the result that blank group minus the fluorescence intensity of the dosing group (1/F
0
–F) has a good linear relationship with the reciprocal of baicalein concentration (1/C), which is consistent with the Lineweaver Burks equation [62], and the linear correlation coefficient R
2 = 0.998.
(1)
1
F 0
− F
= 16 . 42 C + 0.5153
(2)
L O D = 3 N ×
Q I

In eq. (2), LOD is the detection limit, N is the noise value, Q is the sample volume, and I is the signal value, and the detection limit of baicalein can be tested by this formula, which is calculate to be 33 nM. The baicalein solution of 10 μM was detected in parallel for 15 times under optimal detection conditions and the relative standard deviation (RSD) of the detection method was 6.82%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of B-CDs in baicalein sensing
ce:section.ce:para:
Generally, the fluorescence quenching process of quencher-fluorescence CDs system can be attributed to different mechanisms like dynamic quenching, static quenching and/or inner filter effect [63,64]. Dynamic quenching occurs when the intermolecular collisions between the quencher and the fluorescent substance in the excited state causes the fluorescent molecules in the excited state to return to the ground state through energy transfer or charge transfer. This process leads to the sample of fluorescence lifetime changes, and the quenching process conforms to the Stern–Volmer equation (eq. (3)) [65,66]. Static quenching is responsible for the case when the fluorescent substance in the ground state interacts with the quencher to form a non-luminescent complex. During this process, the excited state is not affected with the fluorescence lifetime remains unchanged, which conforms to the Lineweaver-Burks equation (eq. (4)) [65,67]. Inner filter effect means that when the phosphor and light-absorbing substance coexist in the same system, the absorption spectrum of the light-absorbing substance overlaps with the excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of the phosphor, leading to the fluorescence decreases.
(3)
F 0
F
= 1 + C
K
s v
(4)
1
F 0
− F
=
1
F 0
+
K
l b
F 0
C q

In order to confirm the exact quenching mechanisms in B-CDs-baicalein system, several related experiments were carried out. Fig. 8a shows the excitation and emission spectra of B-CDs, as well as the ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum of baicalein. A spectral overlap between the absorption of baicalein and the excitation/emission spectrum of B-CDs could be observed, indicating that the internal filtering effect from baicalein not only competes for the excitation light of B-CDs, but also absorbs part of the emission light of B-CDs, which quenches the fluorescence of B-CDs.

In Fig. 8c, the UV absorption spectrum of B-CDs changed significantly after adding different concentrations of baicalein. In Fig. 8d, after subtracting the UV absorption of baicalein itself, the UV absorption intensity of the B-CDs solution with 10 μM baicalein deviates significantly from the absorption spectrum of the single component B-CDs solution, and the absorption peak becomes wider, indicating that a new complex is formed in the system.

The influence of different concentrations of baicalein in the system on the fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs is also investigated. From Fig. 8b, the fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs can be maintained at 10.53 ns fluctuated in a narrow range of 3.1 % when the concentration of baicalein is 0 μM–30 μM, showing that baicalein has no significant effect on the fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs. Based on the experimental results above and combing the fact that the process of quenching B-CDs fluorescence with different concentrations of baicalein conforms to the Lineweaver-Burks equation, R
2 = 0.998 (Fig. 7b), it can draw a conclusion that the quenching of B-CDs fluorescence caused by baicalein is the combined action of fluorescence internal filtration effect and static quenching effect. The XPS spectrum and IR spectrum have been suggested that B-CDs still retains certain structural characteristics of l-cysteine, which has luxuriant functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, amino, and carboxyl groups on the surface, these functional groups could be essential for the interaction between baicalein and B-CDs as they can provide hydrogen bond donor and hydrogen bond receptor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective ability of B-CDs toward baicalein in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: In practical applications, the testing process often faces interference from a variety of different co-existents to the test. To verify the selectivity and reliability of the method, a series of usual metal ions, carbohydrates and amino acids which are common in biological samples are added to the B-CDs-baicalein system respectively followed by investigation of their fluorescent performance, the test results are shown in Fig. 9 . It can be seen that most of the interfering substances, which are of a relatively large concentration, do not produce obvious interference to B-CDs’ luminescence and thus the detection of baicalein.

The standard addition method [35] was used to verify the feasibility of B-CDs to detect baicalein in biological samples, and the human urine and human blood were employed. As shown in Table 1 , the recovery rate of this detection method in human urine samples is between 97.10 % and 98.19 %, and the recovery rate of human blood samples is between 101.93 % and 103.49 %, proving that the detection method toward baicalein established in this subject is reliable and is expected to be used for practical detection in real sample.

In this work, we have successfully synthesized bright blue emitting CDs (B-CDs) with a considerable QY of 66 % from precursors of cysteine, trisodium citrate dehydrate and ethylenediamine by a simple hydrothermal method. Its particle size is extremely small with average diameter of 3.82 nm and narrow size distribution. Excellent luminescence stability with various pH, salt concentrations, storage time and UV radiation time is found with excitation-independent emission. Based on the good biocompatibility, B-CDs was successfully used for fluorescent imaging of human ovarian cancer cells and the fluorescence can reach the maximum brightness when incubated for 6 h. Moreover, the B-CDs were confirmed to be capable of baicalein sensing with a good linear relationship in range of 0–30 μM and the limit of detection of 33 nM. The corresponding sensing mechanism was proved to be fluorescence internal filtration effect and the static quenching effect. The practical application in real sample was also verified, the recovery rate of this detection method in human urine samples is between 97.10 % and 98.19 %, while between 101.93 % and 103.49 % in human blood samples. All results demonstrate that the B-CDs have good potential of the versatile utilization in biology-relevant fields.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21601081), Guangzhou Scientific planning program (No. 201804010260), Guangxi Natural Science Foundation (No. 2018GXNSFBA281200) and Open project of College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology (QUSTHX201925).
